title: Urticaria — Acute
oneLiner: Transient pruritic wheals (hives) ± angioedema; usually allergic or idiopathic, but must exclude anaphylaxis within minutes.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Anaphylaxis** — the single finding: hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) OR hypoxia (SpO₂ <92%) with urticaria/angioedema → call crash team, give IM adrenaline immediately.
- **Laryngeal oedema** (from angioedema) — the single finding: stridor (high-pitched inspiratory noise) or hoarseness + tongue/uvula swelling → call anaesthetics NOW; do not wait for signs of respiratory failure.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
    - Itchy, red, raised welts (wheals) that appear suddenly, change shape, and migrate over hours.
    - Wheals are pale in centre, erythematous at edge, usually <2 cm but can coalesce.
    - Each wheal resolves within 24 hours (if longer, suspect urticarial vasculitis).
    - ± angioedema: non-pitting swelling of lips, eyelids, tongue, throat, hands, feet.
    - ± burning/stinging pain (more common in urticarial vasculitis).
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Wheals blanch with pressure (urticaria) vs non-blanching (vasculitis).
    - Look for dermatographism (wheal after stroking skin) — common, non-specific.
    - Check for tongue/uvula swelling, stridor, wheeze, hypotension.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
    - **Acute urticaria (idiopathic or allergic)** — no systemic features, resolves <24 h, trigger often identifiable.
    - **Urticarial vasculitis** — wheals last >24 h, painful/burning, leave bruising/pigmentation; low complement levels, positive anti-C1q.
    - **Angioedema without wheals** — consider ACE-inhibitor induced (check drug history), hereditary angioedema (C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency, family history, no itch).
    - **Mastocytosis** — Darier sign (urtication after rubbing lesion), spontaneous wheals, flushing, diarrhoea; rash of telangiectatic macules (urticaria pigmentosa).
    - **Viral exanthem / drug eruption** — often morbilliform rash, fever, systemic symptoms; not solely pruritic wheals.
- **Splitter: “Duration of individual wheal <24 hours” → favours simple urticaria; “>24 hours + pain/bruising” → consider urticarial vasculitis** (check ESR, CRP, complement).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
    - Airway assessment (Mallampati, listen for stridor).
    - Blood pressure, pulse, SpO₂, NEWS2.
    - Skin prick testing (if time allows and trigger suspect) — not routine in acute phase.
- **Bloods:**
    - **If anaphylaxis suspected:** mast cell tryptase (2 samples: 1–2 h after onset, then 24 h later — but don't delay treatment).
    - FBC, ESR, CRP — if suspect vasculitis or infection.
    - Complement C3, C4, C1 esterase inhibitor level/function — if angioedema without urticaria.
    - Thyroid autoantibodies — if chronic course (not urgent for acute).
- **Imaging:** None indicated unless suspect airway compromise → lateral neck X-ray (rarely needed; clinical assessment trumps).
- **Special tests:**
    - Skin prick testing or specific IgE (RAST) — only after resolution, to identify trigger.
    - Challenge testing (e.g., drug provocation) — only in specialist clinic.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
    - **Anaphylaxis definition (NICE):** sudden onset of skin/mucosal changes PLUS one of: respiratory compromise OR hypotension.
    - **Urticaria activity score (UAS7):** used for chronic, not acute.
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway patency assessment** — if stridor or hoarseness, do not order a scan; call for senior/anaesthetic help. Also: **pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain or syncope (to guide workup).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **ABC** — high-flow O₂ if hypoxic, IV access if any systemic symptom.
    - If **anaphylaxis** (hypotension, hypoxia, stridor) → IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) anterolateral thigh; repeat after 5 min if no response. Call crash team.
    - If **mild localised urticaria without angioedema or systemic signs** → oral antihistamine (e.g., loratadine 10 mg or cetirizine 10 mg); observe 1 h.
    - If **urticaria + angioedema of lips/eyes but no stridor/hypotension** → IM adrenaline is still indicated (guidance varies; [check local policy]). Add IV chlorphenamine 10 mg over 2 min, and IV hydrocortisone 200 mg.
    - Remove trigger if identifiable (e.g., stop drug, avoid food).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Continue non-sedating antihistamine regularly (e.g., fexofenadine 180 mg BID or cetirizine 10 mg BID).
    - If angioedema persists after 24 h → consider short course oral steroids (prednisolone 40–50 mg OD for 3–5 days).
    - Monitor for late-phase reaction (recurrence after initial treatment).
    - Admit only if anaphylaxis, severe angioedema, or need for IV therapy.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Avoid trigger (document allergy bracelet if appropriate).
    - Refer to immunology/allergy clinic for recurrent unexplained episodes.
    - Chronic urticaria management: step up antihistamines (up to 4x standard dose), consider montelukast, omalizumab.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 500–1000 mL stat if hypotensive, then maintenance (1.5 L/day).
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if weight >50 kg, eGFR >30) unless bleeding risk.
    - **Diet:** NBM until airway assessed if stridor; else normal.
    - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 15 min until stable, then hourly for 4 h, then 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Non-sedating oral antihistamines:** Loratadine 10 mg PO OD; Cetirizine 10 mg PO OD; Fexofenadine 180 mg PO OD. For severe: can increase to 10 mg BID (cetirizine) or 180 mg BID (fexofenadine) — [check local policy].
    - **Acute severe (IV route):** Chlorphenamine (Piriton) 10 mg IV/IM over 2 min, then 10–20 mg PO over 24 h.
    - **Anaphylaxis — adrenaline:** IM 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000); repeat after 5 min if needed.
    - **Corticosteroids:** Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV stat (100 mg if child), then Prednisolone 40–50 mg PO OD for 3–5 days.
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Sedating antihistamines (diphenhydramine, promethazine) — only if sedation needed (e.g., pruritus interfering with sleep). Avoid in elderly/frail.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Sedating antihistamines: avoid with CNS depressants, in prostate hypertrophy, glaucoma.
    - Adrenaline: caution in ischaemic heart disease, thyrotoxicosis; still give if anaphylaxis.
    - Steroids: avoid in active infection; use with caution in diabetes (monitor glucose).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Cetirizine: reduce dose in moderate-severe renal impairment (e.g., 5 mg OD if eGFR <30).
    - Fexofenadine: no adjustment needed in renal failure (less renal excretion).
    - Loratadine: no adjustment needed in mild-moderate hepatic impairment; caution in severe.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Giving IV adrenaline instead of IM** — IV adrenaline is only for cardiac arrest by an experienced team; IM route is safer and effective for anaphylaxis. Check adrenaline concentration (1:1000 for IM, 1:10,000 for IV). Wrong concentration or route can cause fatal arrhythmia.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Anaphylaxis (can be fatal within minutes).
    - Airway obstruction from laryngeal oedema.
    - Delayed anaphylaxis (biphasic reaction up to 12 h after initial resolution).
- **Chronic:**
    - Progression to chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) — manage with specialist.
    - Psychological distress (sleep deprivation, anxiety, depression).
    - Associated autoimmune conditions (thyroid disease, SLE) in chronic urticaria.
sections.Prognosis: - Most acute urticaria resolves spontaneously within days; >50% are isolated episodes. If recurrent or persists >6 weeks, evaluate for chronic urticaria (often idiopathic, may respond well to high-dose antihistamines). Mortality is almost entirely due to unrecognised anaphylaxis or airway obstruction.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do document** the time of onset and duration of each wheal — this differentiates simple urticaria from vasculitis.
- **Do trial** non-sedating antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine 20 mg OD) if standard dose fails — up-dosing is safe and often effective.
- **Do write** an allergy bracelet / give a written plan (e.g., antihistamine to keep at home) after a confirmed trigger.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe** oral steroids routinely for mild urticaria — they delay diagnosis of chronic urticaria and have side effects. Reserve for severe angioedema or anaphylaxis.
- **Do not rely** solely on sedating antihistamines for daytime control — they impair driving and cognition.
- **Do not discharge** a patient with tongue/lip swelling without observing for at least 6 hours — biphasic anaphylaxis can occur.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - Difficulty breathing, stridor, hoarseness, or feeling of throat tightness.
    - Swelling of lips, tongue, or face.
    - Widespread hives that do not respond to antihistamine within 1 hour.
    - Signs of anaphylaxis (dizziness, collapse, wheeze).
- **Follow-up:**
    - GP in 2 weeks if urticaria persists.
    - Allergy clinic (phone within 1 week if trigger unknown or recurrent episodes).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) immediately if:**
    - Stridor, hypoxia (SpO₂ <92%), hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg).
    - Any sign of anaphylaxis despite initial IM adrenaline.
    - NEWS2 ≥5 or persistent tachycardia >100 despite fluids.
    - Patient on drugs that cause angioedema (ACE inhibitors, NSAIDs) — consider withholding.
- **Call crash team (2222):** cardiac arrest, respiratory arrest, or impending airway obstruction.

rawMarkdown:
# Urticaria — Acute

**One-liner:** Transient pruritic wheals (hives) ± angioedema; usually allergic or idiopathic, but must exclude anaphylaxis within minutes.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Anaphylaxis** — the single finding: hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) OR hypoxia (SpO₂ <92%) with urticaria/angioedema → call crash team, give IM adrenaline immediately.
- **Laryngeal oedema** (from angioedema) — the single finding: stridor (high-pitched inspiratory noise) or hoarseness + tongue/uvula swelling → call anaesthetics NOW; do not wait for signs of respiratory failure.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
    - Itchy, red, raised welts (wheals) that appear suddenly, change shape, and migrate over hours.
    - Wheals are pale in centre, erythematous at edge, usually <2 cm but can coalesce.
    - Each wheal resolves within 24 hours (if longer, suspect urticarial vasculitis).
    - ± angioedema: non-pitting swelling of lips, eyelids, tongue, throat, hands, feet.
    - ± burning/stinging pain (more common in urticarial vasculitis).
- **Key exam findings:**
    - Wheals blanch with pressure (urticaria) vs non-blanching (vasculitis).
    - Look for dermatographism (wheal after stroking skin) — common, non-specific.
    - Check for tongue/uvula swelling, stridor, wheeze, hypotension.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
    - **Acute urticaria (idiopathic or allergic)** — no systemic features, resolves <24 h, trigger often identifiable.
    - **Urticarial vasculitis** — wheals last >24 h, painful/burning, leave bruising/pigmentation; low complement levels, positive anti-C1q.
    - **Angioedema without wheals** — consider ACE-inhibitor induced (check drug history), hereditary angioedema (C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency, family history, no itch).
    - **Mastocytosis** — Darier sign (urtication after rubbing lesion), spontaneous wheals, flushing, diarrhoea; rash of telangiectatic macules (urticaria pigmentosa).
    - **Viral exanthem / drug eruption** — often morbilliform rash, fever, systemic symptoms; not solely pruritic wheals.
- **Splitter: “Duration of individual wheal <24 hours” → favours simple urticaria; “>24 hours + pain/bruising” → consider urticarial vasculitis** (check ESR, CRP, complement).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
    - Airway assessment (Mallampati, listen for stridor).
    - Blood pressure, pulse, SpO₂, NEWS2.
    - Skin prick testing (if time allows and trigger suspect) — not routine in acute phase.
- **Bloods:**
    - **If anaphylaxis suspected:** mast cell tryptase (2 samples: 1–2 h after onset, then 24 h later — but don't delay treatment).
    - FBC, ESR, CRP — if suspect vasculitis or infection.
    - Complement C3, C4, C1 esterase inhibitor level/function — if angioedema without urticaria.
    - Thyroid autoantibodies — if chronic course (not urgent for acute).
- **Imaging:** None indicated unless suspect airway compromise → lateral neck X-ray (rarely needed; clinical assessment trumps).
- **Special tests:**
    - Skin prick testing or specific IgE (RAST) — only after resolution, to identify trigger.
    - Challenge testing (e.g., drug provocation) — only in specialist clinic.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
    - **Anaphylaxis definition (NICE):** sudden onset of skin/mucosal changes PLUS one of: respiratory compromise OR hypotension.
    - **Urticaria activity score (UAS7):** used for chronic, not acute.
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway patency assessment** — if stridor or hoarseness, do not order a scan; call for senior/anaesthetic help. Also: **pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain or syncope (to guide workup).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **ABC** — high-flow O₂ if hypoxic, IV access if any systemic symptom.
    - If **anaphylaxis** (hypotension, hypoxia, stridor) → IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) anterolateral thigh; repeat after 5 min if no response. Call crash team.
    - If **mild localised urticaria without angioedema or systemic signs** → oral antihistamine (e.g., loratadine 10 mg or cetirizine 10 mg); observe 1 h.
    - If **urticaria + angioedema of lips/eyes but no stridor/hypotension** → IM adrenaline is still indicated (guidance varies; [check local policy]). Add IV chlorphenamine 10 mg over 2 min, and IV hydrocortisone 200 mg.
    - Remove trigger if identifiable (e.g., stop drug, avoid food).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Continue non-sedating antihistamine regularly (e.g., fexofenadine 180 mg BID or cetirizine 10 mg BID).
    - If angioedema persists after 24 h → consider short course oral steroids (prednisolone 40–50 mg OD for 3–5 days).
    - Monitor for late-phase reaction (recurrence after initial treatment).
    - Admit only if anaphylaxis, severe angioedema, or need for IV therapy.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - Avoid trigger (document allergy bracelet if appropriate).
    - Refer to immunology/allergy clinic for recurrent unexplained episodes.
    - Chronic urticaria management: step up antihistamines (up to 4x standard dose), consider montelukast, omalizumab.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 500–1000 mL stat if hypotensive, then maintenance (1.5 L/day).
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if weight >50 kg, eGFR >30) unless bleeding risk.
    - **Diet:** NBM until airway assessed if stridor; else normal.
    - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 15 min until stable, then hourly for 4 h, then 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Non-sedating oral antihistamines:** Loratadine 10 mg PO OD; Cetirizine 10 mg PO OD; Fexofenadine 180 mg PO OD. For severe: can increase to 10 mg BID (cetirizine) or 180 mg BID (fexofenadine) — [check local policy].
    - **Acute severe (IV route):** Chlorphenamine (Piriton) 10 mg IV/IM over 2 min, then 10–20 mg PO over 24 h.
    - **Anaphylaxis — adrenaline:** IM 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000); repeat after 5 min if needed.
    - **Corticosteroids:** Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV stat (100 mg if child), then Prednisolone 40–50 mg PO OD for 3–5 days.
- **Common alternatives:**
    - Sedating antihistamines (diphenhydramine, promethazine) — only if sedation needed (e.g., pruritus interfering with sleep). Avoid in elderly/frail.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Sedating antihistamines: avoid with CNS depressants, in prostate hypertrophy, glaucoma.
    - Adrenaline: caution in ischaemic heart disease, thyrotoxicosis; still give if anaphylaxis.
    - Steroids: avoid in active infection; use with caution in diabetes (monitor glucose).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - Cetirizine: reduce dose in moderate-severe renal impairment (e.g., 5 mg OD if eGFR <30).
    - Fexofenadine: no adjustment needed in renal failure (less renal excretion).
    - Loratadine: no adjustment needed in mild-moderate hepatic impairment; caution in severe.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
    - **Giving IV adrenaline instead of IM** — IV adrenaline is only for cardiac arrest by an experienced team; IM route is safer and effective for anaphylaxis. Check adrenaline concentration (1:1000 for IM, 1:10,000 for IV). Wrong concentration or route can cause fatal arrhythmia.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Anaphylaxis (can be fatal within minutes).
    - Airway obstruction from laryngeal oedema.
    - Delayed anaphylaxis (biphasic reaction up to 12 h after initial resolution).
- **Chronic:**
    - Progression to chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) — manage with specialist.
    - Psychological distress (sleep deprivation, anxiety, depression).
    - Associated autoimmune conditions (thyroid disease, SLE) in chronic urticaria.

### Prognosis
- Most acute urticaria resolves spontaneously within days; >50% are isolated episodes. If recurrent or persists >6 weeks, evaluate for chronic urticaria (often idiopathic, may respond well to high-dose antihistamines). Mortality is almost entirely due to unrecognised anaphylaxis or airway obstruction.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do document** the time of onset and duration of each wheal — this differentiates simple urticaria from vasculitis.
- **Do trial** non-sedating antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine 20 mg OD) if standard dose fails — up-dosing is safe and often effective.
- **Do write** an allergy bracelet / give a written plan (e.g., antihistamine to keep at home) after a confirmed trigger.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe** oral steroids routinely for mild urticaria — they delay diagnosis of chronic urticaria and have side effects. Reserve for severe angioedema or anaphylaxis.
- **Do not rely** solely on sedating antihistamines for daytime control — they impair driving and cognition.
- **Do not discharge** a patient with tongue/lip swelling without observing for at least 6 hours — biphasic anaphylaxis can occur.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - Difficulty breathing, stridor, hoarseness, or feeling of throat tightness.
    - Swelling of lips, tongue, or face.
    - Widespread hives that do not respond to antihistamine within 1 hour.
    - Signs of anaphylaxis (dizziness, collapse, wheeze).
- **Follow-up:**
    - GP in 2 weeks if urticaria persists.
    - Allergy clinic (phone within 1 week if trigger unknown or recurrent episodes).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (Registrar/Consultant) immediately if:**
    - Stridor, hypoxia (SpO₂ <92%), hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg).
    - Any sign of anaphylaxis despite initial IM adrenaline.
    - NEWS2 ≥5 or persistent tachycardia >100 despite fluids.
    - Patient on drugs that cause angioedema (ACE inhibitors, NSAIDs) — consider withholding.
- **Call crash team (2222):** cardiac arrest, respiratory arrest, or impending airway obstruction.
